claim: Specialized self-validating agents require hooks (pre-tool-use, post-tool-use, on-stop) embedded in prompts, sub-agents, and skills to validate work deterministically after each tool call, not through global instruction injection
actionable: Define post-tool-use hooks for each specialized agent that validate outputs (e.g., CSV format validation, HTML well-formedness) before returning control; use deterministic validation scripts
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: Focused specialized agents (doing one task well) with tight validation loops outperform generalist agents at scale (across tens, hundreds, millions of runs); a 20-line CSV validator beats a 100-task agent
actionable: Build narrow agents (e.g., CSVEdit agent only edits CSVs) with tight validation; parallelize via sub-agents rather than expanding a single agent's scope
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: Sub-agents enable parallelization of specialized validators: deploy one CSV-edit agent per file in parallel, all validate their work simultaneously via hooks, improving throughput and allowing observability per agent
actionable: For batch operations (editing multiple files), spawn one sub-agent per item with hooks; this enables parallel validation without losing trust guarantees
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: Closed-loop agentic workflows guarantee validation runs automatically; post-tool-use hooks run after every tool call; stop hooks run at workflow completion; this is more reliable than post-hoc prompting (asking agent to validate)
actionable: Never rely on agent-initiated validation prompts; always embed hooks to guarantee validation runs every time without exception
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: Observability via logging is critical for understanding agent behavior; every validator must output logs (timestamped, tagged by agent/file) to debug failures and demonstrate successful validation
actionable: Add logging to every hook script; tag logs with agent context (agent_id, file_path, timestamp); review logs after runs to build confidence in agent quality
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: The core four (context, model, prompt, tools) is foundational to agentic engineering; when learning new agent features, read documentation deeply and understand the underlying mechanics rather than delegating learning to agents
actionable: Invest time in understanding agent frameworks (hooks, workflows, validation patterns) directly; do not outsource learning to agents—you will not scale as an engineer
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING

claim: Vibe coding democratizes development but lacks rigor; agentic engineering requires understanding workflows, validation patterns, and control mechanisms—the difference is knowing vs guessing what your agents do
actionable: Progress from vibe coding (hoping it works) to agentic engineering by reading docs, understanding hooks/workflows, building validation loops, and validating outputs consistently
source_title: The Claude Code Feature Senior Engineers KEEP MISSING
